metadata.title: Why an Accurate Model Can Still Lie Every Time It Says 99%
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism for AI candidate 10. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/02-probability-uncertainty-and-the-confidence-illusion.md (calibration section only).
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = calibrated region / diagonal / honest zone; CRIMSON = overconfident tail / peel / overclaiming
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no ECE or Brier formulas, no temperature-scaling or softmax mechanics, no human-forecaster calibration tangent — one model, one curve

beats.narration_text: Why an accurate model can still lie about how sure it is.

card.copy:
Why an Accurate Model Can Still
Lie Every Time It Says 99%

beats.narration_text: The vendor hands you a number: ninety-four percent accurate. Every metric on the dashboard is green. You sign off.
When the model says ninety-nine percent confident — does that actually happen ninety-nine times out of a hundred?

props.headline: When the model says ninety-nine percent confident — does that actually…

card.copy: When the model says ninety-nine percent confident — does that actually happen ninety-nine times out of a hundred?

beats.narration_text: Accuracy counts correct labels. Confidence is a separate claim — how certain the model is, not just whether it got the label right.
When the model assigns ninety-nine percent to a case, it is making a promise: ninety-nine of every hundred similar cases should be positive.
Run the model on a held-out set. Collect every case where it reported ninety-nine percent confidence. Then check what actually happened.

props.headline: Run the model on a held-out set. Collect every case where…

beats.image_description: A stream of prediction outputs on a screen, each labeled with a high confidence percentage, against a blurred data-center background

beats.narration_text: There is a diagnostic for this. Stated confidence on one axis, actual frequency of positives on the other. A perfectly calibrated model traces the diagonal.
Sort every prediction into a confidence bin. One bin for the low-confidence calls, one for the medium, one for the high. Each bin collects its cases.
If the model says seventy percent confident on a thousand cases, you want roughly seven hundred of those cases to turn out positive.
Count positives in each bin. Low-confidence bins track the claim. High-confidence bins fall short — outcomes arrive well below the stated number.
Connect those frequencies. The curve tracks the diagonal through the middle — then peels away at the top, where the model overclaims.
A net will say ninety-nine percent when it should say eighty-five, and it will say it fluently.

A medical risk model assigns ninety-nine percent confidence to four hundred patients. Count the outcomes: three hundred ten of those four hundred actually have the condition. That is seventy-seven percent, not ninety-nine. Every one of those four hundred cases, the model said essentially certain. Reality said less than four in five. The score was precise. It was also wrong.
Accuracy measures correct calls. Calibration measures honest ones.

props.headline: Accuracy measures correct calls. Calibration measures honest ones.
